items.title: Definition of menopause and climacteric
items.contentHtml:
Definition of menopause and climacteric

  • Menopause is the permanent cessation of menstruation due to the loss of ovarian follicular function; It is defined retrospectively after 12 consecutive months of amenorrhea.
  • Climaterium is the transition period from the reproductive to the non-reproductive stage, which covers perimenopause and the first years of postmenopause.
  • The natural menopause occurs due to follicular exhaustion; Induced menopause results from bilateral oophorectomy, chemotherapy or pelvic radiotherapy.

items.title: Average age of natural menopause
items.contentHtml:
Average age of natural menopause

  • The average age of natural menopause is 51 years (range 45-55 years).
  • It is considered early menopause if it occurs before 40 years (primary ovarian insufficiency).
  • Late menopause is defined when it occurs after 55 years old and is associated with a higher risk of breast and endometrial cancer.
  • Factors that advance menopause: smoking, nulliparity, low body weight, history of ovarian surgery.

items.title: Climacteric stages (STRAW+10)
items.contentHtml:
Stages of the climacteric (STRAW+10)

  • Perimenopause: begins with irregular cycles (variation >7 days in duration) and ends after 12 months of amenorrhea. Includes early and late menopausal transition.
  • Menopause: point defined by the last menstrual period, only identifiable retrospectively after 12 months without menstruation.
  • Postmenopause: period that begins after menopause; It is divided into early (first 5 years) and late (>5 years).
  • The stage -1 (late reproductive) may present elevation of follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) >25 IU/L with normal estradiol.

items.title: Diagnostic criterion: 12-month amenorrhea
items.contentHtml:
Diagnostic criteria: 12-month amenorrhea

  • The diagnosis of menopause is clinical: amenorrhea for 12 consecutive months in the absence of other causes (pregnancy, lactation, thyroid disease, hyperprolactinemia).
  • In women >45 years of age with 12-month amenorrhea, laboratory tests are not required to confirm.
  • In women >45 years of age with 12-month amenorrhea, laboratory tests are not required to confirm.
  • In women 40 years with amenorrhea of 4-6 months, FSH >40 IU/L should be requested in two measurements 1 month apart to diagnose primary ovarian insufficiency.
  • Trickster examination: hormonal contraception can mask amenorrhea; In these cases, FSH should be measured 10-12 days after taking the pill.

items.foco: Vasomotor symptoms: hot flashes and night sweats
items.title: Vasomotor symptoms: hot flashes and night sweats
items.contentHtml:
Vasomotor symptoms: hot flashes and night sweats

  • Vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes and night sweats) affect 75-80% of women in the menopausal transition.
  • Pathophysiology: decrease in estradiol destabilizes the hypothalamic thermoregulatory center, with activation of neurokinin B receptors and kisspeptin.
  • The Greene scale or the Menopause Rating Scale (MRS) quantify the severity.
  • First line in women without contraindications: menopausal hormone therapy (systemic estrogen). Non-hormonal alternative: paroxetine 7.5 mg/day, venlafaxine 37.5-75 mg/day or gabapentin 300-900 mg/day.

items.title: Urogenital atrophy and dyspareunia
items.contentHtml:
Urogenital atrophy and dyspareunia

  • The genitourinary syndrome of menopause (GSM) includes vaginal atrophy, dyspareunia, dryness, pruritus, urinary urgency and recurrent infections.
  • Pathophysiology: estrogen deficiency produces thinning of the vaginal epithelium, a decrease in lactobacilli and an increase in vaginal pH (>5.0).
  • First line: vaginal estrogens (0.01% estradiol cream, estradiol vaginal ring, estriol suppositories).
  • dyspareunia due to atrophy responds well to local estrogens; if there is no improvement, consider lubricants (without hormones) or prasterone (dehydroepiandrosterone) vaginal 6.5 mg/day.
  • Trap: the use of vaginal estrogens does not require associated progestogen (low doses without significant endometrial effect).

items.foco: Risk of osteoporosis due to estrogen deficiency
items.title: Risk of osteoporosis due to estrogen deficiency
items.contentHtml:
Risk of osteoporosis due to estrogen deficiency

  • Estrogen deficiency accelerates osteoclast-mediated bone resorption, with bone mass loss of 2-5% annually in the first 5 years of postmenopause.
  • Osteoporosis is diagnosed with bone densitometry (DEXA) with a T score ≤ -2.5 in the lumbar spine. or hip.
  • Screening indication: women ≥65 years; in those under 65 with risk factors (weight <57 kg, smoking, previous fracture, use of glucocorticoids).
  • Prevention: calcium 1000-1200 mg/day and vitamin D 800-1000 IU/day, weight-bearing exercise.
  • First-line treatment: bisphosphonates (alendronate 70 mg/week, risedronate 35 mg/week).

items.foco: Indications and contraindications of hormone therapy
items.title: Indications and contraindications of hormone therapy
items.contentHtml:
Indications and contraindications of hormone therapy

  • Main indication: relief of moderate to severe vasomotor symptoms in women under 60 years of age or within 10 years of menopause.
  • Other indications: prevention of high-risk osteoporosis if intolerance to bisphosphonates, severe urogenital atrophy.
  • Absolute contraindications: Current or previous breast cancer, endometrial cancer, undiagnosed genital bleeding, active or previous venous thromboembolic disease, acute liver disease, porphyria. Relative contraindications: migraine with aura, hypertriglyceridemia. (>400 mg/dL), systemic lupus erythematosus, established coronary heart disease.
  • In women with a uterus: always combine progestogen (endometrial protection); in hysterectomized women: estrogen only.

items.foco: Management of abnormal uterine bleeding in perimenopause
items.title: Management of abnormal uterine bleeding in perimenopause
items.contentHtml:
Management of abnormal uterine bleeding in perimenopause

  • In perimenopause, abnormal bleeding is defined by changes in frequency, duration, or amount. First evaluation: transvaginal ultrasound to measure endometrial thickness.
  • Normal endometrial thickness in postmenopause: <5 mm. In perimenopause, the threshold is <12 mm in women with bleeding.
  • If thickness ≥12 mm or risk factors (obesity, chronic anovulation), perform endometrial biopsy (Pipelle or hysteroscopy) to rule out hyperplasia or cancer.
  • Medical management: combined oral contraceptives or progestogens cyclical (medroxyprogesterone acetate 10 mg/day for 12-14 days/month).
  • If bleeding persists despite treatment or there are contraindications, consider levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine device or endometrial ablation.

items.foco: Use of SSRI antidepressants for vasomotor symptoms
items.title: Use of SSRI antidepressants for vasomotor symptoms
items.contentHtml:
Use of SSRI antidepressants for vasomotor symptoms

  • Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) and serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs) are non-hormonal options for vasomotor symptoms.
  • First line among SSRIs: paroxetine 7.5 mg/day (FDA-approved fixed dose) or citalopram 10-20 mg/day.
  • SNRI: venlafaxine 37.5-75 mg/day (start with 37.5 mg/day, increase if necessary).
  • Mechanism: modulation of the hypothalamic thermoregulatory center through serotonergic pathways.
  • Trap: paroxetine interferes with metabolism from tamoxifen (CYP2D6 inhibition); in women with breast cancer use venlafaxine or citalopram.

items.foco: Cardiovascular risk and changes in lipid profile
items.title: Cardiovascular risk and changes in lipid profile
items.contentHtml:
Cardiovascular risk and changes in lipid profile

  • Menopause is associated with an atherogenic lipid profile: increase in LDL cholesterol and lipoprotein(a), decrease in HDL cholesterol.
  • Cardiovascular risk increases independently of age, with a higher incidence of coronary heart disease and stroke in the postmenopause.
  • Menopausal hormone therapy initiated in women <60 years or within 10 years of menopause may reduce the risk of coronary heart disease (window of opportunity hypothesis).
  • In women >60 years or within 10 years of menopause, hormone therapy is not recommended for prevention cardiovascular.
  • Assess cardiovascular risk with SCORE scale or Framingham and start statins according to guidelines if LDL >190 mg/dL or high risk.

items.foco: Laboratory tests: FSH and estradiol in menopause
items.title: Laboratory tests: FSH and estradiol in menopause
items.contentHtml:
Laboratory tests: FSH and estradiol in menopause

  • In menopause, follicle stimulating hormone (FSH) rises (>40 IU/L) due to loss of the negative feedback of estradiol.
  • Serum estradiol decreases (<20 pg/mL) due to exhaustion follicular.
  • They are not routinely required to diagnose menopause in women >45 years with 12-month amenorrhea.
  • Indications for measurement: in women <40 years with suspected primary ovarian insufficiency, or in those with hysterectomy or use of contraceptives to confirm menopausal status.
  • The anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) <0.2 ng/mL is a marker of low ovarian reserve and can predict the proximity of menopause.
